Advanced Research Tool

Significance Testing Tool

Choose the type of data you want to test. Each module uses the correct statistical formula for your data type.

📊
Percentage Testing

Test whether the difference between two or more proportions/percentages is statistically significant. Ideal for survey responses, response rates, and categorical data.

z = (p₁ − p₂) ÷ √[(p₁(1−p₁)/n₁) + (p₂(1−p₂)/n₂)]
→
📐
Mean Score Testing

Test whether the difference between two or more mean scores is statistically significant. Requires standard deviation (S.D.) for each group. Ideal for rating scales and Likert data.

z = (m₁ − m₂) ÷ √[(SD₁²/n₁) + (SD₂²/n₂)]
→

How to read the summary table
B
UPPERCASE — significantly higher than B at 95% (z ≥ 1.960)
b
lowercase — significantly higher at 90% only (z ≥ 1.645)
–
Dash — no significant difference at any selected level

🧠
Pairwise Z-Score Matrix
Detailed z-scores and significance for every pair
Run the test to see the matrix
📘
How It Works
Formula and methodology
📐
Formula
z = (p₁ − p₂) ÷ √[(p₁(1−p₁)/n₁) + (p₂(1−p₂)/n₂)]
where p = proportion (%) and n = base size.
🔠
Three-tier notation
B = sig. at 99% · B = sig. at 95% · b = sig. at 90% only. Each figure shows the strongest level achieved.
📊
Thresholds
90% → z ≥ 1.645
95% → z ≥ 1.960
99% → z ≥ 2.576
SIGNIFICANCE TESTING TOOL
📐 Mean Score Testing

Pairwise Z-Score Matrix
Detailed z-scores and significance for every pair
Run the test to see the matrix
📘
How It Works
Formula and methodology
📐
Formula
z = (m₁ − m₂) ÷ √[(SD₁²/n₁) + (SD₂²/n₂)]
where m = mean, SD = standard deviation, n = base size.
🔠
Three-tier notation
B = sig. at 99% · B = sig. at 95% · b = sig. at 90% only. Each figure shows the strongest level achieved.
📊
When to use S.D.
Standard deviation measures score spread within each group. Higher S.D. means more variation, which reduces the z-score and makes significance harder to achieve.
💡
Typical use case
Likert scale ratings (1–5 or 1–10), satisfaction scores, NPS, attribute ratings, and any continuous numeric scale measured across different groups.
⚠️
Requirements
All three inputs (n, mean, S.D.) must be valid. S.D. must be greater than 0. Base size must be a positive integer.
📋
Thresholds
90% → z ≥ 1.645
95% → z ≥ 1.960
99% → z ≥ 2.576
